ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Chana Masala; FOODON_03311737 (chickpea dish) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chana Masala as a prepared curry dish; A0CKP (Chickpeas, cooked) is the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chana Masala or chickpea curry as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chana Masala is approximately 250 g, served as a main dish with rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Chana Masala (Chickpea Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for cooked chickpea curry (chole/chana masala). Values represent a typical home-style preparation with oil, tomato, onion, and spice base. Per-100g values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference for chickpea curry (FDC ID 2346404) used as cross-validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chickpeas, boiled (Cicer arietinum)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 33 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (Cicer arietinum), which is the primary ingredient of Chana Masala. Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283 reports GI=33 (low) for chickpeas. The curry preparation (tomato, onion, spice base, oil) does not substantially alter the GI of the chickpea base. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (33 × 41.25) / 100 ≈ 13.6 for a 250 g serving (16.5 g carb per 100 g × 2.5).

hypertension.guidance: Chana Masala contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 33 and 4.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Chana Masala produces a low glycemic response and supports postprandial blood glucose control, making it a suitable legume-based dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High fibre, low saturated fat, plant-based protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chana Masala provides 4.2 g dietary fibre and only 0.6 g saturated fat per 100 g, with plant-based protein from chickpeas; regular consumption of legume-based dishes is associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk per evidence-based dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chana Masala (also known as Chole Masala or Chhole Masala) is a popular North Indian legume-based curry made from kabuli chickpeas (white chickpeas) cooked in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish originates from the Punjab region of India and Pakistan and is a staple of Punjabi cuisine, widely consumed across South Asia and in Indian diaspora communities worldwide. Preparation involves simmering pre-soaked or canned chickpeas in a base of sautéed onions, garlic, ginger, and tomatoes, seasoned with cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, amchur (dried mango powder), and chili. The result is a thick, tangy, and mildly spicy curry with a hearty texture. Nutritionally, Chana Masala is rich in plant-based protein (approximately 5.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (4.2 g per 100 g), iron, folate, and magnesium. It has a low glycemic index of approximately 33, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is typically served with rice, bhatura (fried bread), puri, or roti. In Singapore, Chana Masala appears on menus at Indian and Middle Eastern restaurants, including Turkish-Lebanese establishments that incorporate South Asian dishes. Clinically, it is a heart-healthy, diabetes-friendly option due to its high fibre content, low saturated fat, and low GI profile.
common_user_queries: Is chana masala good for diabetics?
How many calories in chana masala?
What are the ingredients in chana masala?
Is chana masala high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for cooked chickpea curry, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central. No single exact entry for restaurant-style Chana Masala exists in a primary approved source; values represent a typical home/restaurant preparation. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas as the primary ingredient. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability in oil and salt content across restaurant preparations.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was listed as 'Turkish' based on the field survey restaurant (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine); however, Chana Masala is an Indian dish of Punjabi origin. The cuisine field has been corrected to 'Indian' with region_of_origin 'India (Punjab)'. The restaurant serves a mixed menu including South Asian dishes. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegan dish containing no animal products; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant-based foods. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero per source. 7. GI of 33 is based on boiled chickpeas from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); this is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement for Chana Masala as a prepared dish was found in the approved GI database. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. 9. Aliases consistency verified: all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
